chief_complaint: An older woman presented to the emergency department after an unwitnessed loss of consciousness, with persistent altered mental status.
history: An older woman without seizure history presented to the emergency department after an unwitnessed loss of consciousness, with persistent altered mental status. She had been taking long-term vonoprazan, a potassium-competitive acid blocker (P-CAB).
exam: Vital signs and physical examination findings not provided.
labs: Initial tests showed profound hypomagnesemia (0.6 mg/dL) with low ionized calcium; lactate declined rapidly. Urine showed low magnesium with a fractional excretion of magnesium (FEMg) ≤2.8 %. Perfusion MRI with arterial spin labeling (ASL) showed right-hemispheric hyperperfusion, while conventional sequences showed only a chronic cerebellar infarct. Initial inpatient electroencephalogram showed diffuse low-amplitude slowing without definite epileptiform discharges; by Day 5 the background was more organized. On Day 8, single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) showed reduced perfusion in the previously hyperperfused region. QTc peaked at 603 ms and shortened as electrolytes normalized.
